solution: ```class Solution:
    def findUnsortedSubarray(self, nums: List[int]) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        if n < 2:
            return 0
        
        left, right = 0, n - 1
        
        # Find the first out-of-order element from the left
        while left < n - 1 and nums[left] <= nums[left + 1]:
            left += 1
        
        # If the array is already sorted
        if left == n - 1:
            return 0
        
        # Find the first out-of-order element from the right
        while right > 0 and nums[right] >= nums[right - 1]:
            right -= 1
        
        # Find the min and max in the subarray nums[left..right]
        sub_min = min(nums[left:right + 1])
        sub_max = max(nums[left:right + 1])
        
        # Extend the left boundary to include any numbers > sub_min
        while left > 0 and nums[left - 1] > sub_min:
            left -= 1
        
        # Extend the right boundary to include any numbers < sub_max
        while right < n - 1 and nums[right + 1] < sub_max:
            right += 1
        
        return right - left + 1
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The algorithm identifies the smallest unsorted subarray by first finding the initial out-of-order boundaries, then expanding these boundaries to include any elements that would be out of place after sorting the initial subarray. This achieves O(n) time with constant space by making a few linear passes through the array.
